title: Probability & Statistics
blurb: Unit-by-unit practice for the Probability & Statistics course — counting to inference, every unit with its own exercise set.

units.title: Counting Principles
units.blurb: The multiplication and addition principles, complementary counting, and organized lists — how to count without counting.

units.title: Permutations & Arrangements
units.blurb: Factorials, arrangements of all or some objects, repeated letters, and seating with restrictions.

units.title: Combinations
units.blurb: Choosing without order: nCr, committees, at-least and at-most counts, and when order matters vs when it doesn't.

units.title: Pascal's Triangle & the Binomial Theorem
units.blurb: The triangle that counts everything: binomial coefficients, expanding powers, and finding specific terms.

units.title: Probability Models
units.blurb: Sample spaces, events, equally-likely outcomes, complements, and the addition rule with Venn diagrams.

units.title: Conditional Probability & Independence
units.blurb: How information changes chance: P(A|B), the multiplication rule, independence, weighted trees, and Bayes by table.

units.title: Random Variables & Expected Value
units.blurb: Turning outcomes into numbers: probability distributions, expected value, variance, and what makes a game fair.

units.title: The Binomial Distribution
units.blurb: Repeated independent trials: binomial probabilities, expected count np, shape, and simulation.

units.title: Describing Data
units.blurb: Center and spread done right: mean, median, IQR, standard deviation, boxplots, and the 1.5×IQR outlier fence.

units.title: Distribution Shape & Position
units.blurb: Histograms and shape, percentiles, z-scores, and the normal curve with the 68–95–99.7 rule.

units.title: Two-Variable Data
units.blurb: Scatterplots, the correlation coefficient, the least-squares line, and why correlation is not causation.

units.title: Sampling, Studies & Inference
units.blurb: Random sampling, bias, experiments vs observation, simulation-based inference, and margin of error — the capstone.

forms.title: The multiplication principle
forms.skill: Independent choices multiply: each option pairs with every other.

variants.statement: A menu offers $3$ soups, $4$ main dishes, and $2$ desserts. How many different three-course meals — one soup, one main, one dessert — are possible?
variants.explanation: Each soup pairs with every main and every dessert, so multiply: $3 \cdot 4 \cdot 2 = 24$. Adding $3 + 4 + 2 = 9$ counts single dishes, not full meals.

variants.statement: A menu offers $2$ soups, $5$ main dishes, and $3$ desserts. How many different three-course meals — one soup, one main, one dessert — are possible?

forms.title: Add or multiply?
forms.skill: OR means add the counts; AND (one of each) means multiply.

variants.statement: A locker code consists of one letter chosen from $4$ letters followed by one digit chosen from $10$ digits. How many different codes are possible?
variants.explanation: The code needs a letter AND a digit, so the choices multiply: $4 \cdot 10 = 40$. Adding $4 + 10 = 14$ is the classic slip — that counts picking just one symbol.

variants.statement: A locker code consists of one letter chosen from $5$ letters followed by one digit chosen from $6$ digits. How many different codes are possible?
variants.explanation: The code needs a letter AND a digit, so the choices multiply: $5 \cdot 6 = 30$. Adding $5 + 6 = 11$ is the classic slip — that counts picking just one symbol.

forms.title: Permutations nPr
forms.skill: Order matters: n!/(n−r)! counts arrangements.

variants.statement: Compute $P(5, 2)$ — the number of ways to arrange $2$ of $5$ distinct objects in a row.
variants.explanation: $P(5, 2) = \frac{5!}{(5 - 2)!} = 20$. Dividing by $2!$ as well gives $\binom{5}{2} = 10$ — that counts unordered choices, not arrangements.

variants.statement: Compute $P(5, 3)$ — the number of ways to arrange $3$ of $5$ distinct objects in a row.
variants.explanation: $P(5, 3) = \frac{5!}{(5 - 3)!} = 60$. Dividing by $3!$ as well gives $\binom{5}{3} = 10$ — that counts unordered choices, not arrangements.

forms.title: Arranging people in a row
forms.skill: n people in a row: n!. Fixing one position leaves (n−1)!.

variants.statement: In how many different ways can $3$ people stand in a row for a photo?
variants.explanation: All $3$ people are ordered, so the count is $3! = 6$. Using $(3 - 1)! = 2$ is the circular-table formula — a row has no rotation to cancel.

variants.statement: In how many different ways can $4$ people stand in a row for a photo?
variants.explanation: All $4$ people are ordered, so the count is $4! = 24$. Using $(4 - 1)! = 6$ is the circular-table formula — a row has no rotation to cancel.

variants.statement: In how many different ways can $5$ people stand in a row for a photo?
variants.explanation: All $5$ people are ordered, so the count is $5! = 120$. Using $(5 - 1)! = 24$ is the circular-table formula — a row has no rotation to cancel.

variants.statement: In how many different ways can $6$ people stand in a row for a photo?
variants.explanation: All $6$ people are ordered, so the count is $6! = 720$. Using $(6 - 1)! = 120$ is the circular-table formula — a row has no rotation to cancel.

variants.statement: In how many different ways can $7$ people stand in a row for a photo?
variants.explanation: All $7$ people are ordered, so the count is $7! = 5040$. Using $(7 - 1)! = 720$ is the circular-table formula — a row has no rotation to cancel.

variants.statement: In how many different ways can $8$ people stand in a row for a photo?
variants.explanation: All $8$ people are ordered, so the count is $8! = 40320$. Using $(8 - 1)! = 5040$ is the circular-table formula — a row has no rotation to cancel.

variants.statement: In how many different ways can $3$ people stand in a row for a photo if one particular person must stand first?
variants.explanation: The first spot is forced, so only the other $2$ people are arranged: $(3 - 1)! = 2$. Answering $3! = 6$ ignores the restriction.

variants.statement: In how many different ways can $4$ people stand in a row for a photo if one particular person must stand first?
variants.explanation: The first spot is forced, so only the other $3$ people are arranged: $(4 - 1)! = 6$. Answering $4! = 24$ ignores the restriction.

variants.statement: In how many different ways can $7$ people stand in a row for a photo if one particular person must stand first?

forms.title: Combinations nCr
forms.skill: Order ignored: divide the arrangements by r!.

variants.statement: Compute $\binom{5}{2}$ — the number of ways to choose $2$ objects from $5$ when order does not matter.
variants.explanation: $\binom{5}{2} = \frac{5!}{2!\,3!} = 10$. Forgetting to divide by $2!$ gives $P(5, 2) = 20$ — that counts ordered picks twice-plus over.

forms.title: Committees from two groups
forms.skill: Choose from each group separately, then multiply the counts.

variants.statement: A class has $5$ boys and $6$ girls. In how many ways can a committee of exactly $2$ boys and $3$ girls be chosen?
variants.explanation: Choose the boys and the girls separately, then multiply: $\binom{5}{2}\binom{6}{3} = 10 \cdot 20 = 200$. Using $\binom{11}{5} = 462$ ignores the exactly-$2$-boys condition.

variants.statement: A class has $6$ boys and $5$ girls. In how many ways can a committee of exactly $3$ boys and $2$ girls be chosen?
variants.explanation: Choose the boys and the girls separately, then multiply: $\binom{6}{3}\binom{5}{2} = 20 \cdot 10 = 200$. Using $\binom{11}{5} = 462$ ignores the exactly-$3$-boys condition.

forms.title: Reading Pascal's triangle
forms.skill: Entry k of row n is C(n, k); the whole row sums to 2^n.

variants.statement: What is the entry in position $2$ (counting from $0$) of row $5$ of Pascal's triangle?
variants.explanation: Row $5$, position $2$ is $\binom{5}{2} = 10$. Counting positions from $1$ instead of $0$ lands on $\binom{5}{1} = 5$.

variants.statement: What is the entry in position $2$ (counting from $0$) of row $6$ of Pascal's triangle?
variants.explanation: Row $6$, position $2$ is $\binom{6}{2} = 15$. Counting positions from $1$ instead of $0$ lands on $\binom{6}{1} = 6$.

variants.statement: What is the entry in position $3$ (counting from $0$) of row $6$ of Pascal's triangle?
variants.explanation: Row $6$, position $3$ is $\binom{6}{3} = 20$. Counting positions from $1$ instead of $0$ lands on $\binom{6}{2} = 15$.

variants.statement: What is the entry in position $2$ (counting from $0$) of row $7$ of Pascal's triangle?
variants.explanation: Row $7$, position $2$ is $\binom{7}{2} = 21$. Counting positions from $1$ instead of $0$ lands on $\binom{7}{1} = 7$.

variants.statement: What is the entry in position $3$ (counting from $0$) of row $7$ of Pascal's triangle?
variants.explanation: Row $7$, position $3$ is $\binom{7}{3} = 35$. Counting positions from $1$ instead of $0$ lands on $\binom{7}{2} = 21$.

variants.statement: What is the entry in position $3$ (counting from $0$) of row $8$ of Pascal's triangle?
variants.explanation: Row $8$, position $3$ is $\binom{8}{3} = 56$. Counting positions from $1$ instead of $0$ lands on $\binom{8}{2} = 28$.

variants.statement: What is the sum of all the entries in row $5$ of Pascal's triangle?
variants.explanation: Each row's entries sum to a power of two: row $5$ sums to $2^{5} = 32$. Halving to $16$ comes from using $2^{4}$ — an off-by-one on the row number.

variants.statement: What is the sum of all the entries in row $6$ of Pascal's triangle?
variants.explanation: Each row's entries sum to a power of two: row $6$ sums to $2^{6} = 64$. Halving to $32$ comes from using $2^{5}$ — an off-by-one on the row number.

forms.title: The complement rule

variants.statement: The probability that it rains tomorrow is $\frac{2}{5}$. What is the probability that it does not rain?
variants.explanation: Rain and no-rain are complements, so subtract from one: $1 - \frac{2}{5} = \frac{3}{5}$. Answering $\frac{2}{5}$ again forgets the complement entirely.

variants.statement: A fair die is rolled once. What is the probability of not rolling a $1$?

forms.title: Independent AND
forms.skill: Independent events: multiply. Adding gives OR (minus the overlap).

variants.statement: Events $A$ and $B$ are independent, with $P(A) = \frac{1}{2}$ and $P(B) = \frac{2}{3}$. Find $P(A \cap B)$.
variants.explanation: Independent events multiply: $P(A \cap B) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3}$. The formula $P(A) + P(B) - P(A)P(B) = \frac{5}{6}$ computes $P(A \cup B)$ — the OR, not the AND.

forms.title: Conditional probability from counts

variants.statement: A school has $40$ students; $16$ of them play chess, and $6$ of the chess players are girls. One chess player is chosen at random. What is the probability that a girl is chosen?
variants.explanation: The draw is from the $16$ chess players only, so $P = \frac{6}{16} = \frac{3}{8}$. Dividing by all $40$ students, $\frac{3}{20}$, uses the wrong total — the condition shrank the sample space.

forms.title: Expected value
forms.skill: E(X) = Σ value · probability — a weighted average, not a plain one.

variants.statement: A random variable $X$ takes the values $0$, $2$, and $5$ with probabilities $\frac{1}{4}$, $\frac{1}{4}$, and $\frac{1}{2}$ respectively. Find $E(X)$.
variants.explanation: Weight each value by its probability: $E(X) = 0 \cdot \frac{1}{4} + 2 \cdot \frac{1}{4} + 5 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 3$. Averaging the three values equally, $\frac{7}{3}$, ignores the weights.

variants.statement: A random variable $X$ takes the values $1$, $3$, and $6$ with probabilities $\frac{1}{6}$, $\frac{1}{3}$, and $\frac{1}{2}$ respectively. Find $E(X)$.

forms.title: Exactly k successes
forms.skill: P = C(n,k) p^k (1−p)^(n−k) — the C(n,k) counts the orders.

variants.statement: A fair coin is flipped $3$ times. What is the probability of getting exactly $1$ heads?
variants.explanation: There are $\binom{3}{1} = 3$ orders for the heads among $2^{3} = 8$ equally likely outcomes, so $P = \frac{3}{8}$. Answering $\frac{1}{8}$ forgets the $\binom{3}{1}$ orderings.

variants.statement: A fair coin is flipped $3$ times. What is the probability of getting exactly $2$ heads?
variants.explanation: There are $\binom{3}{2} = 3$ orders for the heads among $2^{3} = 8$ equally likely outcomes, so $P = \frac{3}{8}$. Answering $\frac{1}{8}$ forgets the $\binom{3}{2}$ orderings.

variants.statement: A fair die is rolled $3$ times. What is the probability that it shows a five or a six on exactly $1$ of the rolls?
variants.explanation: Each roll succeeds with $p = \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}$, so $P = \binom{3}{1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{1}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{4}{9}$. Swapping the exponents gives $\frac{2}{9}$ — success and failure traded places.

forms.title: Mean and median
forms.skill: Mean: sum ÷ count. Median: middle of the SORTED list.

variants.statement: Find the mean of the data set: $7$, $2$, $9$, $4$, $3$.
variants.explanation: Add and divide by the count: $\frac{7 + 2 + 9 + 4 + 3}{5} = 5$. The middle value of the sorted list, $4$, is the median — a different statistic.

forms.title: How a new value moves the mean
forms.skill: New mean = (old sum + new value) ÷ (n + 1).

variants.statement: The mean of $4$ numbers is $10$. After one more number, $20$, is added to the list, what is the new mean?
variants.explanation: The old numbers total $4 \cdot 10 = 40$, so the new mean is $\frac{40 + 20}{5} = 12$. Averaging just $10$ and $20$ pretends the list held a single number.

variants.statement: The mean of $5$ numbers is $8$. After one more number, $14$, is added to the list, what is the new mean?

forms.skill: Range = max − min; Q1 is the median of the lower half.

variants.statement: Find the range of the data set: $12$, $3$, $7$, $18$, $5$.
variants.explanation: Range is largest minus smallest: $18 - 3 = 15$. Reporting the maximum $18$ alone confuses the spread with the biggest value.

forms.title: Mean from a frequency table
forms.skill: Weight each value by its frequency before dividing.

variants.statement: On a quiz, $3$ students scored $5$ points, $5$ students scored $7$ points, and $2$ students scored $10$ points. Find the mean score.
variants.explanation: Weight each score by how many students earned it: $\frac{3 \cdot 5 + 5 \cdot 7 + 2 \cdot 10}{10} = 7$. Averaging just the three score values, $\frac{22}{3}$, ignores the frequencies.

forms.title: Reading a scatterplot's trend
forms.skill: Upward cloud → positive; downward → negative; no slope → none.

variants.statement: A scatterplot compares hours a student studies ($x$) with the score they earn on the exam ($y$). As $x$ increases, $y$ tends to INCREASE. Which best describes the association?
variants.explanation: Here both variables rise together, so the cloud of points slopes upward. A real scatterplot is rarely a PERFECT line — association describes the overall trend, not an exact fit.

variants.statement: A scatterplot compares a car's age in years ($x$) with its resale value ($y$). As $x$ increases, $y$ tends to DECREASE. Which best describes the association?
variants.explanation: Here $y$ falls as $x$ rises, so the cloud of points slopes downward. A real scatterplot is rarely a PERFECT line — association describes the overall trend, not an exact fit.

variants.statement: A scatterplot compares a student's shoe size ($x$) with their score on a history quiz ($y$). As $x$ increases, $y$ shows NO CLEAR PATTERN. Which best describes the association?
variants.explanation: Here knowing $x$ tells you nothing about $y$; the points form no sloped cloud. A real scatterplot is rarely a PERFECT line — association describes the overall trend, not an exact fit.

variants.statement: A scatterplot compares daily temperature ($x$) with the number of visitors at a swimming pool ($y$). As $x$ increases, $y$ tends to INCREASE. Which best describes the association?

variants.explanation: Here $y$ falls as $x$ rises, so the cloud of points slopes downward. A real scatterplot is rarely a PERFECT line — association describes the overall trend, not an exact fit.

forms.title: Predicting with a fitted line
forms.skill: Substitute x into ŷ = ax + b — slope times x, plus the intercept once.

variants.statement: A line of best fit for a scatterplot is $\hat{y} = 3x + 4$. Predict $\hat{y}$ when $x = 5$.
variants.explanation: Substitute the $x$-value into the line: $3 \cdot 5 + 4 = 19$. The slope multiplies $x$; the intercept is added once, not multiplied.

forms.title: What kind of study is this?
forms.skill: Assigning → experiment; watching → observational; everyone → census; a subset → sample survey.

variants.statement: A researcher randomly ASSIGNS half of 60 plants to a new fertilizer and half to none, then compares growth. This is best described as…
variants.explanation: Here the researcher ASSIGNS the treatment — that active assignment is exactly what makes it an experiment. Assignment → experiment; watching → observational; everyone → census; a chosen subset → sample survey.

variants.statement: A doctor reviews EXISTING hospital records to compare recovery times of two treatments patients already chose. This is best described as…
variants.explanation: Here no treatment is assigned; existing behavior is only watched or looked up. Assignment → experiment; watching → observational; everyone → census; a chosen subset → sample survey.

variants.statement: The school asks EVERY SINGLE student in the building which lunch option they prefer. This is best described as…
variants.explanation: Here EVERY member of the group is measured, not a subset. Assignment → experiment; watching → observational; everyone → census; a chosen subset → sample survey.

variants.statement: A pollster RANDOMLY SELECTS 400 of a city's voters and asks whom they support. This is best described as…
variants.explanation: Here a random SUBSET of the population answers — a sample standing in for the whole. Assignment → experiment; watching → observational; everyone → census; a chosen subset → sample survey.

variants.statement: A lab randomly ASSIGNS volunteers to sleep 6 or 8 hours, then measures reaction time. This is best described as…
variants.explanation: Here the researcher ASSIGNS the treatment — that active assignment is exactly what makes it an experiment. Assignment → experiment; watching → observational; everyone → census; a chosen subset → sample survey.

variants.statement: A biologist WATCHES wild birds and records their feeding habits without interfering. This is best described as…
variants.explanation: Here no treatment is assigned; existing behavior is only watched or looked up. Assignment → experiment; watching → observational; everyone → census; a chosen subset → sample survey.

variants.statement: The census bureau contacts EVERY SINGLE household in the district to count residents. This is best described as…
variants.explanation: Here EVERY member of the group is measured, not a subset. Assignment → experiment; watching → observational; everyone → census; a chosen subset → sample survey.

variants.explanation: Here a random SUBSET of the population answers — a sample standing in for the whole. Assignment → experiment; watching → observational; everyone → census; a chosen subset → sample survey.

forms.title: From sample to population
forms.skill: Scale the sample proportion up to the population size.

variants.statement: A city has $12000$ residents. In a random sample of $200$ residents, $45$ approve of a new park. What is the best estimate of the TOTAL number of residents who approve?
variants.explanation: Scale the sample rate up to the population: $\frac{45}{200} \cdot 12000 = 2700$. The raw count $45$ describes only the sample, not the whole city.
